OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A newborn with ambiguous genitalia and multiple congenital anomalies was admitted in March 2023. Born preterm at 34 weeks, the infant faced respiratory distress, electrolyte imbalances, and sepsis.
 Imaging Findings: The patient presented with extensive congenital anomalies, including an omphalocele with liver and bowel herniating through a membrane-covered defect, bladder exstrophy, and imperforate anus.
 
 Spinal imaging revealed fusion defects in the lower thoracic and lumbar vertebrae, with an associated cystic mass with dystrophic neural elements. Cranial imaging showed a low-lying torcular Herophili, ventriculomegaly, fullness of the foramen magnum, and possible aqueductal stenosis. Other imaging findings included ectopic kidneys (one partially thoracic and another pelvic), midgut malrotation, sacral agenesis, rib synostosis, pubic symphysis diastasis, ambiguous genitalia, and other musculoskeletal abnormalities, highlighting extensive multi-system involvement.
 
 Due to the patient’s medical instability and eventual demise, as well as lack of resources, further imaging could not assess other abnormalities including undescended testes and detailed evaluation of the urinary system. These structural abnormalities posed considerable diagnostic and management challenges.

DifferentialDiagnosisList: VACTERL association, Caudal regression syndrome, Pentalogy of Cantrell, Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome, OEIS (omphalocele, cloacal exstrophy, imperforate anus, and spinal dysraphism) complex
FinalDiagnosis: OEIS (omphalocele, cloacal exstrophy, imperforate anus, and spinal dysraphism) complex

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 34-year-old female patient presented with exertional dyspnoea, weakness, abdominal pain, and lower limb swelling persisting for a month. There was no history of fever. Initial ultrasound investigation revealed mild ascites, multiple small abdominal lymphadenopathies and minimal pericardial effusion. Laboratory results showed haemoglobin of 12.2g/dL, platelet count of 105×109/L and white blood cell count of 3.32×10
 
 9
 
 /L. A 12-lead ECG revealed prolonged P waves, abnormal Q waves, poor R wave progression, and T wave abnormalities (Figure 1).
 Imaging Findings: Echocardiography revealed mild pericardial effusion, grade II diastolic dysfunction, right ventricle with segmental aneurysmal dilation and apical retraction, mild mitral regurgitation, and hyperechoic mass at the left ventricular apex, indicative of fibrofatty tissue deposition. While the right ventricle (RV) showed localised enlargement due to aneurysm formation, the overall restrictive physiology remained evident (Figures 2a and 2b). No thrombus was detected on echocardiography and cardiac MRI. Chest X-ray showed an enlarged cardiac silhouette and elevated diaphragms.
 
 The patient was referred for a cardiac MRI in our department. Cardiac MRI, done with cine imaging, perfusion, and early and late gadolinium-enhanced (LGE) PSIR sequences on MAGNETOM Vida 3T scanner revealed ascites, pericardial effusion, enlarged right atrium, retraction of the RV apex (RV apical notch), RV aneurysm, obliteration of the left ventricular (LV) apex on True FISP (true fast imaging with steady-state free precession) cine imaging (Figure 3). The LV end-diastolic volume was 69 mL, and the end-systolic volume was 28 mL. Similarly, the RV end-diastolic volume measured 70 mL, with an end-systolic volume of 39 mL, indicating relatively small chamber volumes. LGE imaging demonstrated subendocardial fibrosis with fat infiltration at both the LV and RV apices, resulting in a V-shaped enhancement pattern in each ventricle (Figures 4a, 4b, 4c, and 4d).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 11-year-old male presented to the emergency department at our institution with the chief complaints of insidious, throbbing frontal headache, dizziness, photo-phonophobia and emesis for one week. Neurological examination revealed a positive Kernig sign, ataxia and impaired finger-to-nose testing.
 Imaging Findings: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the head demonstrated a diffuse, heterogeneous solid-cystic lesion in the right inferomedial cerebellar hemisphere involving the adjacent vermis. The solid component was fairly defined with irregular margins, manifesting as mild expansion of the cerebellar folia with abnormal low T1 (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d) and high T2 FLAIR signal interspersed with thick T2 dark striations (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c). The lobulated cystic component showed high signal on T2WI, intermediate on T1WI and incomplete FLAIR suppression without diffusion restriction.
 
 Post-contrast images demonstrated nodular and linear enhancement within the folia and peripheral enhancement of the cyst (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d). Mass effect in the posterior fossa included mild perilesional oedema and partial effacement of the fourth ventricle. There was no evident enlargement of supratentorial ventricles or periventricular oedema. We observed an inferior cerebellar tonsillar descent of 7 mm.
 
 CT showed thick, linear striated calcifications mimicking a “tiger-striped” appearance. Bony remodelling with scalloping of the overlying occipital bone suggested an indolent long-standing lesion (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c).
PostDescription:
Clinical History: An 11-year-old male presented to the emergency department at our institution with the chief complaints of insidious, throbbing frontal headache, dizziness, photo-phonophobia and emesis for one week. Neurological examination revealed a positive Kernig sign, ataxia and impaired finger-to-nose testing.
 Imaging Findings: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the head demonstrated a diffuse, heterogeneous solid-cystic lesion in the right inferomedial cerebellar hemisphere involving the adjacent vermis. The solid component was fairly defined with irregular margins, manifesting as mild expansion of the cerebellar folia with abnormal low T1 and high T2 FLAIR signal interspersed with thick T2 dark striations. The lobulated cystic component showed high signal on T2WI, intermediate on T1WI and incomplete FLAIR suppression without diffusion restriction. Post-contrast images demonstrated nodular and linear enhancement within the folia and peripheral enhancement of the cyst. Mass effect in the posterior fossa included mild perilesional oedema and partial effacement of the fourth ventricle. There was no evident enlargement of supratentorial ventricles or periventricular oedema. We observed an inferior cerebellar tonsillar descent of 7 mm. CT showed thick, linear striated calcifications mimicking a “tiger-striped” appearance. Bony remodelling with scalloping of the overlying occipital bone suggested an indolent long-standing lesion.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Dysplastic cerebellar gangliocytoma, Pilocytic astrocytoma, Paediatric-type diffuse low-grade glioma, MAP kinase pathway altered (FGFR1::TACC1 fusion), Oligodendroglioma-like tumour
FinalDiagnosis: Paediatric-type diffuse low-grade glioma, MAP kinase pathway altered (FGFR1::TACC1 fusion)

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: Case 1
 
 An 8-year-old boy presented with recurrent focal seizures since infancy and progressive developmental delay, along with a port-wine stain on the right side of his face, present since birth.
 
 Case 2
 
 A 54-year-old male with a right hemifacial port-wine stain presented with long-standing epilepsy, progressive left-hemiplegia, cognitive decline and migraine-like headaches. Ophthalmologic evaluation revealed visual field defects and glaucoma.
 Imaging Findings: Case 1
 
 Non-contrast CT images of the brain revealed gyriform, curvilinear cortical and subcortical calcifications along the right parieto-occipital region, with a “tram-track” appearance and associated mild cortical atrophy (Figure 1a). Post-contrast CT images demonstrated pial enhancement over the right parieto-occipital cortex, consistent with pial angiomas. There was also enlargement and enhancement of the ipsilateral choroid plexus (Figure 1b). Additionally, homogeneous crescentic enhancement of the choroid was found along the posterior segment of the right globe, consistent with a choroidal angioma (Figure 1c).
 
 Case 2
 
 T2-weighted and FLAIR axial MR images show right cerebral volume loss with diffusely thinned cortex, ex vacuo dilatation of ipsilateral lateral ventricle, and extensive curvilinear cortical hypointensity (Figures 2a and 2b). Axial susceptibility-weighted image (SWI) shows “blooming” of the extensive cortical/subcortical calcifications on the right cerebral hemisphere (Figure 2c). Post-contrast T1-weighted image shows serpentine gyriform enhancement, and enlargement and enhancement of choroid plexus (Figure 2d).
PostDescription:
Clinical History:
 Case 1: An 8-year-old boy presented with recurrent focal seizures since infancy and progressive developmental delay, along with a port-wine stain on the right side of his face, present since birth.
 Case 2: A 54-year-old male with a right hemifacial port-wine stain presented with long-standing epilepsy, progressive left-hemiplegia, cognitive decline and migraine-like headaches. Ophthalmologic evaluation revealed visual field defects and glaucoma.
 
 Imaging Findings:
 Case 1: Non-contrast CT images of the brain revealed gyriform, curvilinear cortical and subcortical calcifications along the right parieto-occipital region, with a “tram-track” appearance and associated mild cortical atrophy. Post-contrast CT images demonstrated pial enhancement over the right parieto-occipital cortex, consistent with pial angiomas. There was also enlargement and enhancement of the ipsilateral choroid plexus. Additionally, homogeneous crescentic enhancement of the choroid was found along the posterior segment of the right globe, consistent with a choroidal angioma.
 Case 2: T2-weighted and FLAIR axial MR images show right cerebral volume loss with diffusely thinned cortex, ex vacuo dilatation of the ipsilateral lateral ventricle, and extensive curvilinear cortical hypointensity. Axial susceptibility-weighted image shows “blooming” of the extensive cortical/subcortical calcifications on the right cerebral hemisphere. Post-contrast T1-weighted image shows serpentine gyriform enhancement, and enlargement and enhancement of the choroid plexus.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Meningioangiomatosis, Blue rubber bleb nevus syndrome, Wyburn-Mason syndrome, Sturge–Weber syndrome, type 1, PHACE syndrome
